title: Computer Science
intro: Questions on the Computer Science unit draw most heavily on core algorithms and data structures, with strings, pointers, recursion, and Turing machines among the single most frequent answerlines. Sorting algorithms (quicksort, mergesort), graph algorithms (Dijkstra's, depth-first search), and canonical structures like trees, heaps, stacks, and hash tables recur across nearly every batch, reflecting how much of the canon is built on the standard undergraduate algorithms curriculum. Theory of computation supplies a closely related cluster — NP, NP-complete, Big O notation, the halting problem, and the Boolean satisfiability problem — probing the formal boundaries of what algorithms can efficiently solve.

Programming languages and paradigms form a second major cluster, spanning widely used languages (Python, Java, JavaScript, C, C++) as well as functional languages and concepts (Haskell, LISP, monads, lambda calculus, currying) that get asked about disproportionately relative to their real-world popularity. Systems topics — threads, caches, CPUs, operating systems, TCP/IP, and concurrency primitives like semaphores and locks — cover the hardware and infrastructure layer beneath the language layer, while databases contribute a smaller, well-defined cluster built around SQL, relational joins, and transactional consistency.

Artificial intelligence and machine learning make up a growing share of the canon, from classical techniques (decision trees, support vector machines, genetic algorithms) to the neural network concepts increasingly central to the field (attention, overfitting, stochastic gradient descent, convolution). Cryptography and security, and graphics and compression, each supply smaller specialized clusters — hash functions and one-way functions on one side, ray tracing and Huffman coding on the other. Finally, a steady stream of answerlines are historical figures and organizations: Alan Turing, Donald Knuth, Edsger Dijkstra, Claude Shannon, John von Neumann, and John McCarthy recur as common-link answers tying together the algorithms, machines, and languages that bear their names, alongside companies like Google, Microsoft, and Intel.

sections.name: Theory of Computation & Complexity
sections.blurb: Formal models of computation, complexity classes, and the limits of what can be computed.

entries.note: Abstract computational model of a tape and read/write head, defining the limits of what can be computed.
Complexity class of decision problems whose solutions can be verified in polynomial time; central to the P vs NP question.
Notation describing an algorithm's asymptotic time or space complexity as input size grows.
Class of the hardest problems in NP, to which all other NP problems can be reduced in polynomial time.
Computers exploiting superposition and entanglement of qubits, promising speedups for problems like factoring.
Problem of determining whether a Boolean formula can be made true; the first problem proven NP-complete.
Formal system for expressing computation via function abstraction and application, foundational to functional programming.
Symbol of Big O notation, describing an algorithm's asymptotic upper-bound complexity.
Describes languages recognized by finite automata and expressed via regular expressions.
Undecidable problem of determining whether an arbitrary program will terminate, proved by Turing.
Abstract computational model of a tape and read/write head defining the limits of computability.
Central resource measured in algorithmic complexity analysis, as in time complexity.
Algebraic system of true/false values and operations (AND, OR, NOT), foundational to digital logic.
Study of the time and space resources required to solve computational problems.
Formal rules defining a language's syntax, classified by the Chomsky hierarchy.
Quantum algorithm providing a quadratic speedup for unstructured search problems.
Diagrammatic method for simplifying Boolean algebra expressions in digital logic design.
Describes complexity or time growing proportionally with input size.
Common asymptotic time complexity of efficient comparison-based sorting algorithms like mergesort.
Complexity class of decision problems solvable in polynomial time.
Describes algorithms that use random choices to achieve good expected performance or approximate solutions.
Quantum algorithm efficiently factoring large integers, threatening RSA encryption.
NP-hard problem of finding the shortest route visiting a set of cities exactly once and returning to the start.
Data type representing true or false values, foundational to logic and conditional statements.
Formal grammars generating languages recognized by pushdown automata, used to define programming language syntax.
Cellular automaton devised by John Conway where cells live, die, or reproduce based on neighbor rules; Turing-complete.
Problem of determining whether two graphs are structurally identical; its complexity status is not fully resolved.
Operation in formal language theory denoting zero or more repetitions of a pattern, used in regular expressions.
Integers greater than 1 divisible only by 1 and themselves; the hardness of factoring them underlies RSA cryptography.
The Boolean satisfiability problem, the first problem proven NP-complete via the Cook-Levin theorem.
Exclusive-or logical operation returning true when inputs differ, used in cryptography and digital circuits.

sections.name: Algorithms
sections.blurb: Techniques for solving problems efficiently, from sorting and searching to graph and optimization methods.

entries.note: Technique where a function calls itself to solve smaller instances of a problem, requiring a base case.
Operation combining two matrices, optimized by algorithms like Strassen's beyond the naive cubic-time approach.
Divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm using a pivot to partition an array, with average-case O(n log n) time.
Greedy algorithm finding shortest paths from a source vertex to all others in a graph with non-negative weights.
Patterns describing sets of strings, used for searching and matching text.
Technique solving problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems and storing solutions to avoid recomputation.
Optimization technique maximizing or minimizing a linear objective subject to linear constraints.
Graph traversal algorithm exploring as far as possible along each branch before backtracking.
Divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm splitting, sorting, and merging subarrays in O(n log n) time.
Task of arranging data in a defined order, via algorithms like quicksort, mergesort, and heapsort.
Class of graph problems solved by algorithms like Dijkstra's, Bellman-Ford, and Floyd-Warshall.
Optimization technique caching results of expensive function calls to avoid redundant computation.
Decomposition of an integer into its prime factors; its computational hardness underlies RSA cryptography.
Technique for arranging biological sequences to identify regions of similarity, often via dynamic programming.
Task of finding occurrences of a pattern within text, solved by algorithms like KMP and Boyer-Moore.
Shortest-path algorithm handling negative edge weights, slower than Dijkstra's but more general.
Graph problem finding the greatest possible flow from a source to a sink, solved via algorithms like Ford-Fulkerson.
Computational techniques using repeated random sampling to estimate numerical results.
Basic arithmetic operation, optimized in computing via algorithms like Karatsuba's beyond schoolbook methods.
Algorithms that process input piece-by-piece without knowledge of the future, as opposed to offline algorithms.
Task of finding a target element or path, solved via methods like binary search or graph search.
Task of finding a target element or path, solved via methods like binary search or graph search.
Algorithmic technique incrementally building candidate solutions and abandoning ones that fail constraints.
Algorithm finding a target in a sorted array by repeatedly halving the search interval.
Simple sorting algorithm repeatedly swapping adjacent out-of-order elements, with O(n^2) worst-case time.
Computational technique determining when two or more objects intersect, common in graphics and physics simulation.
Algorithmic strategy breaking a problem into subproblems, solving them recursively, and combining the results.
Efficient algorithm computing the discrete Fourier transform in O(n log n) time, widely used in signal processing.
Dynamic programming algorithm computing shortest paths between all pairs of vertices in a graph.
Algorithms making locally optimal choices at each step in hopes of finding a global optimum.
Optimization problem selecting items with given weights and values to maximize value within a capacity constraint.
Divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm splitting, sorting, and merging subarrays in O(n log n) time.
Process of finding the best solution from a set of feasible options according to some objective.
Process of generating sequences of numbers that approximate randomness via deterministic algorithms.
Describes a function or process defined in terms of itself, requiring a base case to terminate.
Methods for arranging data in order, including quicksort, mergesort, and heapsort.

sections.name: Data Structures
sections.blurb: The structures — arrays, trees, hash tables, stacks — used to organize and access data.

entries.note: Sequences of characters manipulated via concatenation, substring, and search operations fundamental to programming.
Structures of vertices connected by edges, traversed via algorithms like depth-first search and Dijkstra's.
Hierarchical structures of nodes with a root and children, including binary search trees and heaps.
Last-in-first-out data structure supporting push and pop operations, used in function call management.
Tree-based structures satisfying the heap property, used to implement priority queues.
Tree-based structure satisfying the heap property, used to implement priority queues; also a region of dynamically allocated memory.
Last-in-first-out structures supporting push and pop operations.
Hierarchical structure of nodes with a root and children.
Fixed-size, indexed collection of elements stored contiguously in memory.
Fixed-size, indexed collections of elements stored contiguously in memory.
Stack structure tracking active function calls and their local variables during program execution.
Sequences of nodes each pointing to the next, allowing efficient insertion and deletion.
Data structure associating keys with values, often implemented via hash tables.
First-in-first-out data structure supporting enqueue and dequeue operations.
Dynamic, resizable arrays that grow to accommodate additional elements.
Self-balancing tree structures maintaining sorted data, widely used in databases and file systems.
Tree structure where each node has at most two children.
Tree structures where each node has at most two children.
Space-efficient probabilistic structure testing whether an element is possibly in a set, with no false negatives.
Occur when two keys hash to the same bucket in a hash table, resolved via chaining or open addressing.
Occur when two keys map to the same slot in a hash table, resolved via chaining or open addressing.
Structure mapping keys to values via a hash function, offering average constant-time lookup.
Structures mapping keys to values via a hash function, offering average constant-time lookup.
Abstract data type where elements are served according to priority, often implemented with a heap.
First-in-first-out data structures supporting enqueue and dequeue operations.
Self-balancing binary search trees maintaining balance via node coloring rules, ensuring O(log n) operations.

sections.name: Programming Languages & Paradigms
sections.blurb: Languages, language features, and the paradigms (functional, object-oriented, imperative) that shape how code is written.

entries.note: Variables storing memory addresses, central to languages like C for direct memory manipulation.
High-level, dynamically typed language emphasizing readability, widely used in data science and scripting.
Programs translating source code into machine code or another target language, via lexing, parsing, and code generation.
Dynamic scripting language central to web browsers, enabling interactive client-side behavior.
Object-oriented, statically typed language running on the Java Virtual Machine, known for "write once, run anywhere."
Low-level, statically typed language developed by Dennis Ritchie, foundational to Unix and systems programming.
Classifications specifying what kind of value a variable holds, e.g. integer, string, or boolean.
Paradigm treating computation as evaluation of mathematical functions, avoiding mutable state, as in Haskell and Lisp.
Control structures repeating a block of code while a condition holds, e.g. for and while loops.
Abstraction in functional programming for sequencing computations with context, e.g. handling side effects in Haskell.
Paradigm organizing code around objects with encapsulated state and behavior, using inheritance and polymorphism.
Process of analyzing a sequence of tokens against a formal grammar, used in compilers and interpreters.
An anonymous function, or the symbol for function abstraction in lambda calculus.
Mechanism for handling runtime errors by transferring control to designated handler code.
Object-oriented feature allowing a class to acquire properties and methods from a parent class.
Object-oriented feature allowing objects of different types to be treated through a common interface.
Systems programming language emphasizing memory safety without a garbage collector, via its ownership model.
Languages like Haskell and Lisp emphasizing immutable data and function composition.
Reusable blocks of code that take inputs and return outputs.
Instances of classes bundling data and behavior in object-oriented programming.
Formal languages for instructing computers, spanning imperative, functional, and object-oriented paradigms.
Programming language and environment designed for statistical computing and graphics.
Describes typing checked at compile time, or variables/methods bound to a class rather than instances.
Typesetting system created by Donald Knuth, widely used for producing mathematical and scientific documents.
Non-executable annotations in source code explaining logic for human readers.
Process of translating source code into machine code or an intermediate representation.
Process of translating source code into machine code or an intermediate representation.
Functional programming technique transforming a multi-argument function into a sequence of single-argument functions.
Object-oriented principle bundling data and methods while restricting direct access to internal state.
Process of computing the value of an expression according to a language's semantics.
Purely functional, lazily evaluated programming language known for its strong static type system and monads.
Markup language structuring content on web pages, interpreted by browsers alongside CSS and JavaScript.
Early functional programming language created by John McCarthy, notable for its parenthesized prefix syntax and code-as-data philosophy.
Minimalist dialect of Lisp emphasizing simplicity and functional programming.
Changes a function makes beyond returning a value, such as modifying state; avoided in pure functional programming.
Sequences of characters processed as strings within programs.
System by which a language classifies values, either statically at compile time or dynamically at runtime.

sections.name: Systems, Architecture & Networking
sections.blurb: Operating systems, hardware architecture, concurrency, and the protocols that connect machines.

entries.note: Independent sequences of execution within a process, enabling concurrency.
Application-layer protocol underlying data communication on the World Wide Web.
Automatic memory management reclaiming memory no longer referenced by a program.
Hardware and abstractions for storing data during computation, from registers and cache to RAM.
Transmission Control Protocol, providing reliable, ordered, connection-based delivery of data over networks.
Small, fast memory storing frequently accessed data to reduce access latency.
Graphics processing units with massively parallel architectures, used for rendering and machine learning workloads.
CPU technique overlapping execution stages of multiple instructions to improve throughput.
Computation performed simultaneously across multiple processors or cores.
Small, fast storage locations within a CPU holding data for immediate processing.
Small, fast memories storing frequently accessed data to reduce access latency.
State where processes block each other indefinitely while waiting for resources held by one another.
Internet Protocol, responsible for addressing and routing packets across networks.
Core component of an operating system managing hardware resources and process scheduling.
Open-source Unix-like operating system kernel created by Linus Torvalds.
State where excessive data traffic degrades network performance, managed via protocols like TCP's congestion control.
Synchronization primitives controlling access to shared resources among concurrent processes.
CPU technique guessing the outcome of conditional branches to keep instruction pipelines full.
Graphics processing unit with a massively parallel architecture, used for rendering and machine learning.
Numerical labels assigned to devices on a network for identification and routing.
Software managing hardware resources and providing services for computer programs.
Network devices that forward data packets between networks based on IP addresses.
Software emulations of computer systems, running an operating system or program in an isolated environment.
Central processing unit executing instructions via fetch-decode-execute cycles.
Techniques detecting and correcting data corruption during storage or transmission, e.g. via Hamming codes.
Named collections of data stored persistently and organized by a file system.
Basic digital circuit storing one bit of state, a building block of registers and memory.
Conditions in pipelined CPUs where the next instruction cannot execute in the following clock cycle.
Interface defining a processor's supported instructions, registers, and data formats, e.g. x86 or ARM.
Core operating system components managing hardware; also functions used in kernel methods for machine learning.
Synchronization mechanisms preventing concurrent processes from simultaneously accessing shared resources.
Low-level commands executed directly by a CPU, encoded in binary.
Software managing hardware resources and providing services for computer programs.
Architectural style for networked applications using stateless HTTP requests to access resources.
Operating system function determining which process or thread runs on the CPU at a given time.
Semiconductor devices that amplify or switch electronic signals, the basic building blocks of digital circuits.
Protocol providing reliable, ordered, connection-based delivery of data over networks.
User Datagram Protocol, a connectionless protocol offering fast but unreliable data delivery.

sections.name: Databases
sections.blurb: Structured data storage and retrieval, from relational schemas to query languages.

entries.note: Organized collections of structured data, queried and managed via systems like SQL-based relational databases.
Databases organizing data into related tables, queried via SQL and structured relationships.
Structured Query Language used to define, query, and manipulate data in relational databases.
SQL operation combining rows from two or more tables based on a related column.
Database property ensuring transactions bring the database from one valid state to another, part of ACID.

sections.name: Artificial Intelligence & Machine Learning
sections.blurb: Systems that learn from data, including neural networks, classic ML models, and natural language processing.

entries.note: Computational models loosely inspired by biological neurons, composed of layered, weighted connections.
Machine learning task assigning inputs to discrete categories based on learned patterns.
Unsupervised learning task grouping similar data points without labeled categories.
Vector of partial derivatives used to update model parameters during training, as in gradient descent.
Failure mode where a model fits training data noise too closely, harming generalization to new data.
Field concerned with building systems that perform tasks requiring human-like intelligence, from search to machine learning.
Field building systems that learn patterns from data rather than through explicit programming.
Neural network mechanism weighting the importance of different input elements, central to transformer models.
Optimization technique inspired by natural selection, evolving candidate solutions via mutation and crossover.
Supervised learning models finding the optimal hyperplane separating classes of data.
Supervised learning models splitting data via a tree of feature-based decisions to reach predictions.
Task of automatically translating text between languages using statistical or neural models.
Computational models of layered, weighted connections loosely inspired by biological neurons.
Machine learning task predicting a continuous output value from input features.
Mathematical operation combining input data with a filter or kernel, core to convolutional neural networks.
Scalars used in dimensionality reduction techniques like principal component analysis.
Statistical models representing systems with unobserved states inferred from observable outputs, used in speech recognition.
Stochastic processes where future states depend only on the present state, used in probabilistic models.
Natural language processing, the field concerned with enabling computers to understand and generate human language.
Optimization algorithm updating model parameters using gradients estimated from small random batches of data.

sections.name: Cryptography & Security
sections.blurb: Techniques for secure communication, encryption, and authentication.

entries.note: Functions mapping data to fixed-size values, used in hash tables and cryptographic integrity checks.
Study of techniques for secure communication, encompassing encryption, hashing, and digital signatures.
Decentralized cryptocurrency using blockchain and proof-of-work to secure transactions without a central authority.
Functions easy to compute but computationally infeasible to invert, foundational to modern cryptography.
Advanced Encryption Standard, a widely used symmetric block cipher securing digital data.
Pieces of information used to encrypt and decrypt data or authenticate access.
Secret strings used to authenticate identity, typically stored as salted cryptographic hashes.
Cryptographic method allowing one party to prove knowledge of a fact without revealing the fact itself.

sections.name: Graphics, Compression & Signal Processing
sections.blurb: Rendering, image representation, and the encoding of signals and data.

entries.note: Techniques for reducing data size, either losslessly, as with Huffman coding, or lossily, as with JPEG.
Rendering technique simulating light rays to produce realistic reflections, shadows, and refractions.
Represented digitally via models like RGB, central to rendering and image processing.
Techniques for reducing data size, either losslessly or lossily.
Lossless data compression algorithm assigning shorter codes to more frequent symbols.
Reversible text transformation that groups similar characters, improving compression algorithms like bzip2.
Digital representations of visual data as grids of pixels, processed and compressed by graphics algorithms.
Smallest addressable elements of a digital image, each holding color information.
Process of generating an image from a model using techniques like rasterization or ray tracing.
Technique determining how light interacts with surfaces to render realistic color and lighting in graphics.

sections.name: People, Companies & Software Engineering
sections.blurb: The figures, firms, and engineering practices that shaped computing.

entries.note: British mathematician who formalized computation via the Turing machine and cracked Nazi Enigma ciphers at Bletchley Park.
Practice of verifying code correctness via unit, integration, and system tests.
Technology company known for its search engine, the PageRank algorithm, and major contributions to distributed systems.
Process of finding and fixing errors in software.
Computer scientist and author of The Art of Computer Programming; created TeX and formalized the analysis of algorithms.
Dutch computer scientist known for the shortest-path algorithm bearing his name and structured programming advocacy.
"Father of information theory" who defined entropy and founded digital circuit design via Boolean algebra.
Distributed version control system created by Linus Torvalds, tracking changes to source code.
American semiconductor company that pioneered microprocessors, including the x86 architecture.
Physicist and computer scientist who created Mathematica and the Wolfram Language, and studied cellular automata.
British mathematician who formalized computation via the Turing machine and cracked Enigma at Bletchley Park.
Russian mathematician whose Markov chains model memoryless stochastic processes used throughout computer science.
Errors or flaws in software causing incorrect or unexpected behavior.
Reusable solutions to common software design problems, e.g. singleton, factory, and observer patterns.
Web-based hosting platform for Git repositories, central to open-source collaboration.
Computer scientist who coined the term "artificial intelligence" and created the Lisp programming language.
Polymath whose von Neumann architecture, describing a stored-program computer, underlies most modern computer design.
Technology company founded by Bill Gates and Paul Allen, known for the Windows and Office software lines.
Observation by Gordon Moore that the number of transistors on a chip doubles roughly every two years.
Linguist whose Chomsky hierarchy classifies formal grammars by generative power, foundational to computer science theory.
Restructuring existing code to improve readability or structure without changing its external behavior.
